params.label: Background
params.notes: This first section sets up the medical problem before any code. Two facts matter: heart risk is built from many measurements at once, and women's heart disease is systematically missed. Both will shape how we read our model later.

params.code:
fw = body_w*0.56
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g5_heart/slides/figures/intro_risk_factor_stack.png', left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=body_w*0.56, max_h=body_h-0.25)
px = body_l + fw + 0.40
pw = body_w - fw - 0.40
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=body_h-0.15, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
gy = body_top + body_h*0.5 - 1.30
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=gy+0.07, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'No doctor diagnoses heart disease from one number', left=px+0.48, top=gy, width=pw-0.70, height=0.60, size=16, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=gy+0.85, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'They combine several routine checkup measurements into a single risk estimate, the way the Framingham Risk Score does. Worldwide, about nine risk factors led by cholesterol and smoking explain over 90% of first heart attacks. Risk is a stack of factors, not a single cause.', left=px+0.48, top=gy+0.78, width=pw-0.70, height=2.10, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: The takeaway is that no single measurement decides heart risk -- doctors add up many factors, the way the Framingham score does. The bars show how much each factor multiplies risk; cholesterol and smoking lead, but none acts alone. Hold this 'stack of factors' idea for when we test cholesterol by itself.
params.title: How doctors actually estimate heart risk

params.code:
fw = body_w*0.56
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g5_heart/slides/figures/intro_symptom_presentation.png', left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=body_w*0.56, max_h=body_h-0.25)
px = body_l + fw + 0.40
pw = body_w - fw - 0.40
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=body_h-0.15, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
gy = body_top + body_h*0.5 - 1.30
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=gy+0.07, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'Here is the problem that drives our project', left=px+0.48, top=gy, width=pw-0.70, height=0.60, size=16, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=gy+0.85, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'The “textbook” heart attack, crushing chest pain down the left arm, is the male picture. Women more often feel back pain, nausea, and shortness of breath, so their heart attacks get missed. The American Heart Association documents that women are misdiagnosed and under-treated more than men.', left=px+0.48, top=gy+0.78, width=pw-0.70, height=2.10, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: This slide is the motivation for the whole project. The classic chest-pain picture is the male one, so women's often different symptoms get missed, and the AHA has documented that women are under-diagnosed and under-treated. That real-world bias is exactly what we will look for inside our own model.
params.title: Women’s heart disease is under-diagnosed

params.label: Methods
params.notes: Now we turn to what we actually built. This section covers the prediction task, the dataset, and the four models we compared -- and why the choice of algorithm turns out to be the least interesting decision of all.

params.code:

gap = 0.35
cw = (body_w - gap*2) / 3.0
ch = body_h

ac = accents[0]

gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98

_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'The Cleveland Heart Disease dataset, from a 1980s study.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
cx = body_l + 1*(cw+gap)
ac = accents[1]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, '13 features', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'Age, sex, blood pressure, cholesterol, chest-pain type, exercise-ECG results.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)

ac = accents[2]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, 'Predict disease', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, '1 = has heart disease, 0 = does not. Graded on held-out patients.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: Our job is a simple yes/no prediction from a routine checkup, using the classic Cleveland dataset: 303 patients, 13 features, graded on patients the model never saw. Because tabular data like this is easy for any model, the real questions are which features matter and who the model works for, not which algorithm wins.
params.title: The task and the data

params.code:
accents = [TURQUOISE_RGB, DEEPPINK_RGB, AMBER_RGB]
gap = 0.35
cw = (body_w - gap*2) / 3.0

_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98

_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'Simple, explainable, and here the best of the four.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
cx = body_l + 1*(cw+gap)
ac = accents[1]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, 'Random Forest & CatBoost', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'Tree ensembles, the workhorses of tabular data.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
cx = body_l + 2*(cw+gap)
ac = accents[2]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, 'TabPFN', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'A foundation model for tables, like the one from Day 2.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: We trained four different models -- a simple logistic regression, two tree ensembles, and a tabular foundation model -- and always compared them to the 'just guess the majority' baseline of about 0.54. Anything that cannot beat that baseline is useless. They all beat it, and they all basically tie.
params.title: Four models, one honest test

params.label: Results
params.notes: Here are the three findings. Watch for a pattern: the flashy model comparison teaches the least, while the two 'boring' experiments -- one feature, and a fairness split -- teach the most.

params.code:
fw = body_w*0.56
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g5_heart/slides/figures/model_comparison.png', left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=body_w*0.56, max_h=body_h-0.25)
px = body_l + fw + 0.40
pw = body_w - fw - 0.40
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=body_h-0.15, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=body_top+body_h*0.5-0.55, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'All four models land between 0.79 and 0.81, comfortably above the coin-flip baseline, and they basically tie each other. That is the lesson: on clean tabular data the model barely matters. Swapping a plain logistic regression for a fancy foundation model buys almost nothing. Three hundred patients from the 1980s contain only so much signal, and no model can invent more.', left=px+0.48, top=body_top+0.30, width=pw-0.70, height=body_h-0.70, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
params.notes: All four models land within a whisker of each other, around 0.80. The lesson is that on clean tabular data the algorithm barely matters -- a plain logistic regression matches a fancy foundation model. The ceiling is set by the data itself, not by how clever the model is.
params.title: The data sets the ceiling

params.code:
fw = body_w*0.56
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g5_heart/slides/figures/feature_ablation.png', left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=body_w*0.56, max_h=body_h-0.25)
px = body_l + fw + 0.40
pw = body_w - fw - 0.40
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=body_h-0.15, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=body_top+body_h*0.5-0.55, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'Everyone “knows” cholesterol causes heart disease, so we gave a model only the cholesterol column. It scored about 0.52, basically a coin flip. Meanwhile the checkup with cholesterol removed scored about 0.80. Cholesterol is a major cause of heart disease over decades, but on its own it is a poor clue for who has disease right now. That is why doctors combine many measurements.', left=px+0.48, top=body_top+0.30, width=pw-0.70, height=body_h-0.70, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
params.notes: We gave one model only the cholesterol column and it scored near a coin flip, while dropping cholesterol entirely barely hurt. Cholesterol matters over decades, but on its own it is a weak clue for who has disease right now -- which is why doctors combine many measurements. This is the 'stack of factors' idea made concrete.
params.title: Cholesterol alone is a weak predictor

params.code:
fw = body_w*0.56
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g5_heart/slides/figures/fairness_by_sex.png', left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=body_w*0.56, max_h=body_h-0.25)
px = body_l + fw + 0.40
pw = body_w - fw - 0.40
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=body_h-0.15, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
gy = body_top + body_h*0.5 - 1.30
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=gy+0.07, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'This is the point of the project', left=px+0.48, top=gy, width=pw-0.70, height=0.60, size=16, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=gy+0.85, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'One accuracy number can hide unfairness. When we grade the model separately for women and men, it is about 0.88 accurate for women and 0.78 for men, a gap of about 0.09. An accuracy gap in either direction means the model behaves differently depending on your sex. You only see it because you looked.', left=px+0.48, top=gy+0.78, width=pw-0.70, height=2.10, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: This is the heart of the project. A single accuracy number looked fine, but grading women and men separately reveals about a nine-point gap. The point is not the direction of the gap -- it is that you only discover unfairness when you deliberately look for it.
params.title: Accurate overall, but not equally by sex

params.label: Being honest
params.notes: Good medical-AI work states loudly what it cannot claim. This section covers where our result comes from -- a small, skewed, decades-old dataset -- and what we are and are not allowed to conclude from it.

params.code:
fw = body_w*0.56
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g5_heart/slides/figures/intro_dataset_sex_skew.png', left=body_l, top=body_top+0.10, max_w=body_w*0.56, max_h=body_h-0.25)
px = body_l + fw + 0.40
pw = body_w - fw - 0.40
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=body_h-0.15, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=px, top=body_top+0.05, width=pw, height=0.09, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
gy = body_top + body_h*0.5 - 1.30
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=gy+0.07, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'A model can only learn from who is in its data, and ours is about 2:1 male', left=px+0.48, top=gy, width=pw-0.70, height=0.60, size=16, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=px+0.24, top=gy+0.85, width=0.11, height=0.11, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'With far fewer women, the model gets less practice on them, and the way patients were selected in the 1980s made the male rows sicker. That skew is not a bug we can fix with a better model, it is a property of the data, and it is exactly the bias that makes heart disease harder to catch in women.', left=px+0.48, top=gy+0.78, width=pw-0.70, height=2.10, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: Our data is about two men for every woman, so the model simply gets less practice on women. That skew is not a bug we can code away -- it is baked into how the 1980s study was collected, and it mirrors the same real-world bias that makes women's heart disease harder to catch. Every result above must be read through this limitation.
params.title: The data is small and male-skewed

params.code:
accents = [TURQUOISE_RGB, DEEPPINK_RGB, AMBER_RGB]
gap = 0.35
cw = (body_w - gap*2) / 3.0
ch = body_h
cx = body_l + 0*(cw+gap)
ac = accents[0]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, 'It can', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'Show that checkup features carry real signal, that no single number is the whole story, and that accuracy can be unequal across sex.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
cx = body_l + 1*(cw+gap)
ac = accents[1]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, 'It cannot', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'Say anything trustworthy about today’s patients. This is 303 people from one 1980s study.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
cx = body_l + 2*(cw+gap)
ac = accents[2]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, 'Watch the metric', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'Missing a real heart attack is far worse than a false alarm. A deployed tool would be tuned for sensitivity, not raw accuracy.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: This is the most important slide in any medical-AI talk. Our toy model earns the right to say checkup features carry signal, no single number is the whole story, and accuracy can be unequal -- but it cannot say anything trustworthy about today's patients. And in a real tool, missing a heart attack is far worse than a false alarm, so you would tune for sensitivity, not raw accuracy.
params.title: What this project can and cannot say

params.label: References
params.notes: Every claim in this deck traces to a peer-reviewed source. These are the papers behind the risk factors, the under-diagnosis of women, and the dataset itself.

cx = body_l + 1*(cw+gap)
ac = accents[1]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))

gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, 'Under-diagnosis in women', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'Mehta 2016, AHA scientific statement, Circulation. van Oosterhout 2020, JAHA. Bugiardini and Bairey Merz 2005, JAMA.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
cx = body_l + 2*(cw+gap)
ac = accents[2]
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=(SURFACE_RGB if SURFACE_RGB else PAPER_RGB))
_add_rect(slide, left=cx, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=ac)
gy = body_top + ch*0.5 - 0.98
_add_text(slide, 'The dataset', left=cx+0.30, top=gy, width=cw-0.60, height=0.60, size=19, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else INK_RGB), font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_rect(slide, left=cx+0.32, top=gy+0.64, width=0.72, height=0.035, fill_rgb=ac)
_add_text(slide, 'Detrano 1989, American Journal of Cardiology. UCI Machine Learning Repository, Heart Disease Data Set.', left=cx+0.30, top=gy+0.86, width=cw-0.60, height=ch*0.5, size=15, color_rgb=(WHITE_RGB if ON_DARK else MUTED_RGB), font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes: These are the primary references. The risk-scoring and INTERHEART work back the 'stack of factors' framing; the AHA statement and its follow-ups document the under-diagnosis of women; and Detrano 1989 is the origin of the Cleveland dataset we used.
params.title: Sources
